---
title: "Getting Started with AI Tools"
subtitle: "AI Bootcamp for UVM Economics — Session 1"
author:
  - Emily Beam
  - Erkmen Aslim
date: 2026-04-22
format:
  revealjs:
    slide-number: c/t
---

# Overview

## Two lunch talks on AI tools

- **Today**: what the tools are, what they actually do, live demos
- **April 27**: research workflows, slide creation, web scraping, automated grading
- No installation needed today — just watch, ask questions, try things later

## We started using these seriously in December

We are still learning. The tools are still changing.

- We started using Claude Code and Codex in December while working on the Economics AI letter
- The learning curve is real, but it is short — a few days of real use, not a semester

<!-- EMILY: Add 2-3 specific things you used them for early on, what surprised you, and what went wrong. The more concrete the better. Examples: "I used it to extract all the due dates from my syllabus into a CSV — took two minutes instead of twenty." "It confidently cited a paper that does not exist." -->

## The AI ladder

Where are you today?

| Level | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| 0 | Haven't tried it |
| 1 | Tried it once, wasn't impressed |
| 2 | Use occasionally for simple tasks |
| 3 | Regular user, integrated into workflow |
| 4 | Power user, multi-step workflows |
| 5 | Building systems with AI |

*Adapted from Kyle Jensen's AI adoption framework, as described in [Goldsmith-Pinkham (2026)](https://paulgp.substack.com/p/getting-started-with-claude-code).*

**Goal today:** show you what Levels 3–4 look like.

## Your students are already using this

This is the elephant in the room.

- Students are using ChatGPT and Claude on your assignments. You know this.
- Understanding what these tools can and cannot do makes your assessment design better.
- This bootcamp is partly about that: if you know the tool, you can design around it.
- This is not a policy talk. But knowing the technology helps you make better policy.

## Things I have actually used this for since December {.smaller}

<!-- EMILY: Star the ones you want to keep, cut anything that feels wrong. This is meant to be overwhelming on purpose. -->

**Research** · Built a replication package — read my do-files, found the dependency chain, wrote the master script · Reproducible balance tables (Stata → LaTeX) · R script to pull IPUMS census data via API, then Stata code to analyze it · Data dictionary from a messy dataset · Adversarial review of my own paper — flagged causal language without identification · Structured literature review with formatted memo

**Teaching** · Course website from scratch (Hugo + GitHub Pages) · Lecture slides in Quarto · Made *these slides* · Assignment prompts and rubrics from learning goals

**Writing** · Created a "voice file" from my real emails — now drafts sound like me · Drafted replies to emails I'd been avoiding · Letter to a journal editor · Announcement email for this bootcamp

**Email** · Inbox triage: categorizes messages by action needed · Reads a thread, I say the gist, it drafts in my voice · Kid's hockey schedule emails → Google Calendar events

**Workflow** · Project hub system — one entry point per project with status and blockers · Reusable saved workflows for weekly review, session planning, literature search · Morning briefing: calendar + tasks + email + deadlines in one view · End-of-session handoff notes so I pick up where I left off

## Things that were fun but saved me zero time

<!-- EMILY: Add/cut freely. The joke is the honesty. -->

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="55%"}
- Built a Vermont cycling route planner *(I have never used a single route)*
- D&D solo campaign (very patient dungeon master)
- Built a project management system instead of doing the project
- Automated my to-do list review — now I procrastinate *faster*
:::
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](assets/vt-routes-screenshot.png){width="100%" .shot}
:::
::::

::: {.aside}
The learning curve is real, but at least the rabbit holes are entertaining.
:::

# Tools

## The useful distinction is chat vs. file access

The brand name matters less than what the tool can actually do.

| Chat-based tools | Agentic tools |
|---|---|
| Ask questions, draft text, summarize an uploaded file | Work directly on files in your project |
| One conversation at a time | Multiple sessions on different tasks |
| You copy-paste content in and out | It reads your Stata do-files, edits them, runs them |
| Each conversation starts fresh | Can be shaped with standing instruction files |
| Easier to start with | More setup, but you get real outputs, not just advice |

::: {.highlight-box}
UVM provides [Microsoft Copilot](https://go.uvm.edu/copilot) for free — good for Office integration, but it does not do the file-access workflows we are focusing on today.
:::

## You can run multiple tasks at once

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](assets/multi-workflow-1.png){width="100%" .shot}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](assets/multi-workflow-2.png){width="100%" .shot}
:::
::::

- Separate tasks into separate sessions instead of piling everything into one thread
- One window might be planning, another writing, another reviewing code
- This is normal, not an advanced edge case

## The big jump is when it can work on your actual files

Instead of copying your syllabus into a chat window, the tool reads it directly from your folder.

- Less copy-paste, easier iteration across many files
- Outputs stay in your project as real files
- You can build workflows you reuse later
- Much better fit for real research and teaching work

# Standing instructions

## You can give the tool standing instructions

For agentic tools, files like `CLAUDE.md` or `AGENTS.md` are loaded automatically when you start working in a project.

- They store stable preferences, permissions, and workflow rules
- They reduce how much you re-explain every session
- You can have general preferences and project-specific ones
- Keep them short — if they get too long, important rules get lost

<!-- EMILY: Replace the TODO below with a real screenshot of your CLAUDE.md or a short text excerpt. If no screenshot, delete the two-column layout and just describe it: "Mine is about 30 lines. It tells the tool: use full file paths, don't rewrite my phrasing, be direct." -->

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="58%"}
_TODO: add updated context/preferences screenshot here._
:::
::: {.column width="42%"}
- Preferences like file paths, permission rules, writing style
- A file like `EB-VOICE.md` stores my writing defaults
- After a work session, update the file based on what you learned
:::
::::

# Context

## The context window is why long conversations go sideways

The context window is the tool's working memory for the current session.

- Every model has a maximum — when it fills up, performance gets worse
- Larger, messier sessions accumulate more mistakes
- This is why you separate tasks into multiple sessions

::: {.alert-box}
For bigger tasks: **plan**, then **execute**, then **review**, then **clear context** and continue. Don't do everything in one long messy thread.
:::

# Safety

## Don't give it your IRB data

Sensitive work can often be redesigned rather than abandoned.

- Do not give it files with PII unless the platform and rules are appropriate
- Let it work on code and log files instead of raw protected data
- Paid subscriptions generally let you turn off training on your content — do that
- The tool can make mistakes, but it will usually pause and ask permission on risky actions
- You can refuse, stop the task, or tighten the rules

# Demos

## What to expect from the demos

You are about to see practical examples, not magic.

- Live prompting — we type a request, the AI responds, we iterate
- Occasional waiting — AI processing is not instant
- Reviewing output critically — the first draft is never the final product
- Slides will be visible during wait times so you can follow along

::: {.highlight-box}
These tools work especially well with GitHub for version control and collaboration, but GitHub is not required for anything we are doing today.
:::

## Wrap-up

The biggest jump in usefulness comes when the AI can work directly with your files.

### Before next time (April 27)

- Try using an AI tool for **one real task** this week
- Install the tools from the [prep guide](https://eabeam.github.io/uvmecon-ai/prep.html) if you want to try on your own
- Think of a research or teaching task you'd like to automate
